review_text: Finally, a movie that treats the legacy of Spider-Man with respect. Bringing back Tobey and Andrew was incredible, but the best part was how it redeemed the previous franchises and fixed their unfinished arcs. It’s a massive, emotional payoff for long-time fans of every era
the multiverse expanding with villains like green goblin, doctor octopus, electro, the lizard, and sandman, alongside the toby Maguire and Andrew Garfield spider men, this movie is just awesome.
It's fan service, but also quality cinema. Always a joy when those two intersect, even though it's rare.
Good movie recommended1111

Stretches a one-shot episode concept to the run-time of a feature-length movie. The movie has pretty low rewatchability and breaks the neat narrative sequence that had been set up in Homecoming + Far From Home; it feels disjointed against the backdrop of the rest of Spiderman's MCU adventures. I think centering the movie around characters from 2 seperate movie franchises is A) asking a lot of the audience and B) not a good way to implement multiverse dynamics. The movie should still revolve around Earth-199999 [MCU] Peter Parker and his reality, rather than the *guests* to his movie, his universe. For me the way Dr Strange's spell worked was kind of lore-breaking. How does he seemingly have control over the entire multiverse? Why is he being rash and reckless when they could sit down to plan a spell beforehand? I liked them interacting, but again, I don't think a spiderman movie should ever build around just one spell Dr Strange did.

Is just me or is marvel just trying to make me feel emotionally broken, this movie is awesome I loved every second of this movie, all the cast from all previous spider man films in one film was a dream come true, but Peter’s arc was altered from him a couple movies ago making references to Star Wars to him going on revenge mode like he’s the freaking punisher. But the ending is so sad and depressing, and that is what makes this movie so special.

So overrated. This film is just straight up nostalgia bait and it comes across more like a parody or fan fiction rather than an actual movie. I’ll admit it’s entertaining and the visuals are pretty good, but in terms of the story and characters, they’re very poorly written and the performances cannot save it.

If I could give zero stars I would… this was truly an awful viewing experience I made a Rotten Tomatoes account just to write this review…Jesus Christ that was painful. Even though I’m 5 years late, I want it etched on the internet for eternity that I saw this half baked, cringey mess for what it really is, Marvel count your motherf*cking days. Your corny one liners were cute in the first few movies but I’ve had ENOUGH. My eyeballs can only see so much of the back of my head while sitting through a singe film. And the storyline???? What are we actually doing here? NONE of Peter Parker’s choices made an ounce of sense. And don’t get me started on Tom Holland and Zendaya’s on-screen chemistry. I literally didn’t even remember they were in a real life relationship until half way through the damn thing. In conclusion, this was a severely overhyped, sorry excuse of a "blockbuster" film with about as much depth to it as a thimble.

Spider-Man: No Way Home, one of the best MCU movies ever. I love that the three Spider-Men, and each of their villains except for Tom Holland. But, the cinematography, the story and the visuals are fantastic. The final battle on the Statue of Liberty will forever be a legendary battle scene. This movie has all the emotions, because you get emotional, you get happy, you get mad, for some reason. The action sequences are so satisfying and entertaining. One of the best theater experiences of all time. But, overall, I give Spider-Man: No Way Home a 10/10 or a 5/5 stars.

Spider-Man: No Way Home is an immensely special Spider-Man movie. A massive fan event where everyone got to see their favorite Spider-Men all together, with mixed timelines and villains and an epic final battle. It’s some of the best fan service of all time. Now, the storytelling is rather messy, particularly in the first half. It’s not very coherent, and the inciting incident is literally just Peter failing to get his friends into college. And of course, the likelihood of our favorite heroes all being sent to the same universe through that spell-gone-wrong is bizarrely unlikely. But ultimately, this is just an extremely satisfying movie. It’s bold and ambitious without losing sight of the heart of these characters, and ends things on a satisfying note.

So far, Spider-Man’s solo films in the MCU until this point had been lackluster, the MCU after Endgame was on a decline in overall quality and cohesiveness and the multiverse had not really kicked off in any meaningful way — thats why it was surprising that this film came out and turned each of those critiques around. One of the most fun Spider-Man films, integration of the multiverse in ways that also played on our nostalgia and a great, high quality film with fantastic performances. Truly, a top 5 marvel film.

This is the only MCU Spider-Man rerelease I wanted to watch in the theatres, and I knew it wouldn't be as good as watching it the first day. I knew a lot of it didn't really make much sense, but the plot really doesn't make any sense at all during the first half. The MIT stuff, the way the law gets involved, was all really stupid, but the core plot of how the redemption of those people is his responsibility is beyond ridiculous. But everything around the plot that gives the momentum still works bloody well. The humour did work for me more than I thought it would this time. I still love all the cameos. I also feel like Willem Dafoe was even better here than in the original Spider-Man. But Andrew Garfield is my favourite part of this movie. I wish he had actually got a chance to conclude his movies. I really liked how they decided to end it, too, even though a lot of it still doesn't make any sense. Among the rereleases, this felt like the one with the least image quality, even though this was the only one that had an IMAX rerelease. Even the actual vfx quality felt poor. I noted it especially about Doc Ock, because Spider-Man 2 was great to watch on the big screen, and this just didn't have that detail in it. It just seems like, due to bad CGI, they had to kind of make everything look a little less clear. Overall, this had a lot of problems, but the second half of the movie worked really well and made it feel more like the earlier superhero films which I enjoyed a lot.

Watched Spider-Man : No Way Home during its re-release in Mumbai theatres for just ₹105. Unlike the earlier Spider-Man portrayals that carried a sense of maturity, this film is clearly designed with teenagers in mind. If the story had followed Doctor Strange’s wisdom, the film could have ended in an hour. However, Peter Parker’s immaturity, indecisiveness, and lack of experience stretch the narrative into a 2.5-hour journey. This highlights that the new Spider-Man is still learning, far less seasoned than his predecessors. It is only through personal loss especially the death of his mother figure that he gains exposure to the harsh realities of life and begins to grow. This emotional turning point pushes him forward, shaping his path as a hero. Overall, I would give this film a 3-star rating.
